with a camera to do lymph node dissection why can't i just go to the right and open the pleura or left and open the pleura and do a lobectomy and so Marcin Zelensky really pushed the envelope for trans cervical lobectomy the reason why these techniques came into being is because intercostal space has an intercostal nerve and no matter how careful you are no matter how well you
take care of the nerve.

So a certain percentage of patients were still getting pain from even those tiny vats that we were putting in.
Hence Marcin decided to go in from the neck and do his surgeries from the neck.
This axis is a good axis when you're dealing with upper lobes.
So doing the right upper lobectomy or left upper lobectomy is okay.
But trying to do a lower lobectomy and trying to do dissection of station 8, station 9 lymph nodes becomes a bit of a challenge.
And the second important thing is you're leaving the patient with a large scar which is visible in a visible area.
So that again, cosmesis is also an issue for the patient.
So that's why trans cervical lobectomy is not taken off in a big way.
But sometimes when you're doing a Tembla or a Vambla, you can actually go to the right or left and do an upper lobectomy.
A guy in Taiwan called C.C.
Lui, who's a very close friend of mine, then said, if we can do this from the neck, similarly at the same time, he said, why can't we do it from the sub-siphon axis?
The advantage of the sub-siphon axis is that you, again, don't go through any of the intercostal nerves, so you can go in very smoothly in a sub-siphon plane.
And the second advantage was the specimen delivery is much more easier in a sub-siphoids plane.
When you come between the nerves, sometimes you do your whole operation with 10 or 12 mm incision, but to take the specimen out, you have to extend the incision a little bit.

with a sub-Z-fied area because there is no ribs to hold the specimen down is much more easier to take the specimen out so that's one advantage and the second advantage is that if you want to do bilateral surgery you can very easily go into the right side and then just turn around and go into the left side so both can be done so very often now my sub-Z my thymectomies
I actually prefer to go in with a sub-z-foil access which gives me access to the right and access to the left side of the pleura if I want a complete radical dissection.
So you can do this and this is again a technique that has come into being and a lot of people are employing this.
The problem with this technique is that you have to use extra long instruments.
Because the hilum is far away from you.
The lymph nodes in station 2, 3, 4 are far away.
So, you know, your manipulation of your instruments becomes a bit more difficult when you are operating at a distance with valves.
And so, you need to adapt your skills to be able to do a sub-Z-foil lobectomy.
Now in the labs in Pennsylvania, I have seen this happen.

People are trying to develop a transumbilical lobectomy, which means around the umbilicus, they make an incision, a periumbilical incision, and they get into there and they dilate what is called as a umbilical vein.
So the falciform ligament, the umbilical vein lies within the falciform ligament.
And actually, if you follow the umbilical vein up, it goes transdiaphragmatic into the chest.

and they have done this in a pig where they made the transumbilical incision and dilated the umbilical vein within the falciform ligament and then gone transdiaphragmatic and I have seen them do small surgeries by this sort of thing but all in a pig model I don't think this technique as yet is taken off in a

in thoracic surgery in a big way just as here but who knows if the instruments get better or if we get into single port robotics and things like that maybe we might go transambulical one of the problems of doing a single port robotic on the chest wall is that the diameter of the single port which i said in my last lecture it is a little bigger because we have got five six instruments coming through that single port
And to place it in the intercostal space, there is a lot of restriction of activity.

So because there is restriction of activity and there is a risk of compression of the intercostal nerve, maybe this technique of going transumbilical or sub-zephaled using the single port robot may actually take place in future.

okay now moment you have done the positioning the one mistake which everybody does is they forget to tell your anesthetist to isolate the lung you must remember that it takes about 15 to 20 minutes for the lung to go down completely and if you ask the anesthetist to isolate the lung after you have done your incision chances are you will puncture the lung when you go in
So once I position, I will ask the anesthetist to isolate the lung prior to draping.
I do it prior to draping so that I go scrub, three minutes scrub, come back, paint, drape.
There is enough time for the chest to go down.
That is the most important thing for me.
